preFix.title: Laying self-adhesive vinyl floor tiles
preFix.subtitle: Peel-and-press vinyl tiles on a flat, clean subfloor in a kitchen, bathroom, or utility room
preFix.excerpt: How to lay self-adhesive vinyl tiles. Covers subfloor preparation, finding the centre of the room, the correct laying sequence, and trimming around edges and obstacles.
preFix.sourceNotes: Collins Complete DIY Manual. Reader's Digest Complete DIY Manual.

content.text: Self-adhesive vinyl tiles are one of the most forgiving flooring jobs. The method is simple and mistakes can be fixed while the tile is still warm. The key is finding the room centre and planning the layout so cut tiles at opposite walls are equal.
Step 1, Prepare the subfloor

The subfloor must be dry, flat, and free of dust, grease, old adhesive, and loose particles. Any undulation greater than 2 mm shows through thin vinyl tiles within months as a visible ridge. Fill dips with floor-levelling compound; sand high spots with the sanding block. On a wooden subfloor, secure any loose boards with screws driven flush, then fix 6 mm hardboard or plywood over the boards to give a level surface. Clean the subfloor thoroughly before tiling.
Step 2, Find the room centre
Find the midpoint of each wall. Snap a chalk line between the two midpoints to create the first
datum line

. Snap a second chalk line at right angles through the centre point. Check the lines are truly perpendicular with a 3-4-5 check: measure 300 mm along one line from the centre, 400 mm along the other, and confirm the diagonal between those two points measures 500 mm exactly.
Step 3, Dry-lay a test row

Lay tiles dry along each datum line from the centre to the walls without peeling the backing. Count the tiles and measure the gap at each wall. If a gap is less than half a tile wide, shift the starting point by half a tile in that direction and snap a new line. Equal edge tiles on opposite sides look intentional; a narrow sliver on one wall and a wide cut on the other looks like a mistake.
Step 4, Lay the field tiles

Warm the first few tiles near a radiator or in a sunny room for 30 minutes, cold tiles are stiff and less adhesive. Peel the backing from one tile, position the corner exactly on the centre point, and press it firmly from the centre outward to push out any air. Apply the rubber mallet to bed the tile flat. Work outward in a pyramid pattern from the centre, keeping edges aligned with the chalk lines. Wipe any adhesive residue from the tile surface with a damp cloth before it sets.
Step 5, Cut and fit edge tiles

To mark an edge tile, lay it face-up on top of the last full tile closest to the wall. Lay a further tile on top of this, butted to the wall. Draw a line along the edge of the top tile onto the tile below, this marks the cut line. Cut through both layers of backing paper and vinyl with the utility knife and a metal straightedge, scored in several passes rather than cut in one. Leave a 2 mm gap at the wall for expansion; skirting board covers it.
Step 6, Cut around obstacles

For curves around pipes or door frames, make a paper template. Fold a piece of newspaper to match the tile size, crease it against the obstacle to mark the cut line, then transfer to the tile and cut. For pipe holes, mark the pipe centre on the tile, cut a circular hole slightly larger than the pipe diameter with a sharp utility knife, then cut a straight slit from the hole to the nearest tile edge so the tile can be slipped around the pipe.
Variations
Diagonal layout: work from the room centre as above but rotate the datum lines to 45°. Tile waste increases by 15 to 20% because every edge row requires cuts at 45°.
Checkerboard in two colours: alternate tiles by colour, working outward from the centre point. Dry-lay the full room before peeling any backing to check the pattern reads as intended.
Care

Avoid heavy floor-cleaning machines. Clean with a damp mop and a pH-neutral cleaner. Self-adhesive tiles can lift at the edges in prolonged damp, run a thin bead of flooring adhesive under any lifted edge and weight it overnight.
Troubleshooter

items.fix: Lift the tile, clean the subfloor and tile back with white spirit, let dry, apply a thin coat of contact adhesive to both surfaces, and press back.
items.cause: Subfloor contamination, or tiles applied cold and not bedded firmly

items.fix: Fill with floor-levelling compound under the affected tiles. Lift tiles while still warm; they can usually be re-used.

initialFindings.message: PATTERN tutorial has no Method/Steps section and no orderedList — sequential instructions missing
tricolon: tricolon (three parallel items) — two adjectives almost always beats three
paragraph reads at grade 11.1 (qc threshold: 11) — simplify (target grade 6-8)
